style: (No longer in collection) Informal upright, twin-trunk
notes: Never had much potential, but could have become a nice, desert-inspired shohin. It rather held sentimental value, as it's been with me for 9 years, having been grown from a very small leaf cutting as an experiment. Lost it in 2026 to either leaf burning, root rot or both. Guess I just don't have any luck with Crassulas.

A jungle in a pot! The disheveled look of my old Ficus after spending a season in a small balcony greenhouse, right after a full defoliation and before major pruning. The first aerial roots started showing up as well. A while before I planted some Ficus pumila vines at the base, for accent but they've conquered the canopy in the lush humidity of the greenhouse. The tree was about 14 years old and 11 years in training here. Looks like I forgot to turn the HDR setting off when I took the shot, but it gives it a dramatic effect; I kind of like it.

First bloom of a Murraya germinated from seed, less than 2 years old at the time. Still in the collection, now transitioning from a semi-cascade to a full cascade style. It has bloomed every winter since, and the fragrance is incredible - this tiny thing can fill up a whole room.

At about 4 years old, the trunk was already gaining character and bending towards a slanting, possibly semi-cascade position. Gave it a better, rusty-crackled pot in 2022, courtesy of Sergey Bogopolski (Сергей Богопольский), Chernivtsi.

Great species for extra-mini bonsai (mame, shito and keshitsubo), not-so-great for bonsai design. It's very drought-tolerant and can thrive with minute quantities of soil and the leaves are quite small. But! It has a bad habit of winter dieback (including mature branches) and tends to form a caudex, quite hard to shape as a bonsai trunk. Still hangs around in my collection, maybe I'll get somewhere with it.

First bloom after its first year of training. The small pot and full sun on a southern windowsill worked like a charm. It's starting to develop some ramification which will become the basis of the future crown, and leans towards a slanting position.

Still blooming every summer, still reaching for the sun, the old Bouga at peak growth. I lost it in 2024. Thinking back, I can't believe how many years it managed to survive in full-indoor conditions.

About 5 years old at the time, it was getting in shape towards a twisted, gnarled, semi-cascade. It never showed too much promise and had weak growth most of the time, but it bloomed every summer. I lost it in 2024. Thinking back, I can't believe how many years it managed to survive in full-indoor conditions.

Also tried my hand with Crassulas while experimenting with succulents. This could have become an interesting savannah-style, mame-size mini bonsai, but nature had other plans and I lost it (and most other Crassulas) to either leaf burning, root rot or both. Not great, not terrible.

Also tried my hand with Crassulas while experimenting with succulents. This could have become an interesting savannah-style, mame-size mini bonsai, but nature had other plans and I lost it (and most other Crassulas) to either leaf burning, root rot or both. Not great, not terrible.

The start of a shito-sized project with a tiny arid species (coin added for size comparison). Due to a lack of any outdoor setting and indoor space, I started experimenting more and more with woody succulents as mini-bonsai around 2017-2020. Some projects were dead-ends from the start, while others still grow well & happy. My soft spot for mini-bonsai and desert plants remained.

First repotting of the old Ficus to a proper bonsai pot, courtesy of Zsolt Demeter (Chimi Bonsai Pots). The tree was already about 11 years old here and the new crown was starting to take shape. Still very weak trunks at the time.

I started this back in 2015 from a very small cutting. It never showed too much promise and had weak growth most of the time, but it bloomed every summer. I lost it in 2024. Thinking back, I can't believe how many years it managed to survive in full-indoor conditions.
Same old Ficus, here after a repot and complete structural restart (only main branches kept).

One of my earliest 'bonsai' attempts that still hangs around in my collection. Shot taken right after the initial 'styling', from a typical supermarket bush. Almost died twice, got several complete makeovers, the trunks and lower main branches are all that remain from that time. Can't wait to show how the tree evolved after 14+ years.

My very first attempt at 'bonsai', collected during spring, 2009. Here pictured a year later in its wooden training pot. The wiring work is, eh... less than ideal. :) I planted it back to nature 1 or 2 years later, realising it's never going to become a bonsai. The original photo was taken by a 3.2 Mpx camera of a Sony Ericsson K800i, so I had to sharpen it a bit using Gemini.
